title: Enhancing User Authentication with Wearable Data: Evaluating Sampling Strategies and Classifiers Using Apple Watch Sensor Logs
venue: 18th International Conference on COMmunication Systems & NETworkS (COMSNETS)

method.input_representation: Combination of statistical (mean, std, var, skew, kurt), time-domain (RMS, correlation, ZCR), and pedometer features (total steps, mean pace, cadence, distance)
method.training_strategy: One-vs-Rest (OvR) framework using StratifiedShuffleSplit (80/20) and GridSearchCV for hyperparameter tuning. Evaluated SMOTE, Random Under-sampling (RUS), and weight-balancing.

evaluation.context: Achieved with XGBoost using S7 feature set; reported as fewer than 1 in 1,000 impostors falsely accepted even with 100 users

evaluation_protocol: 5-fold cross-validation; one-vs-rest classification; group size scaled from 10 to 100 users

reported_limitations: Class imbalance inherent in one-vs-rest framework
Trade-off between usability (TAR/FRR) and security as the user group size increases

usability.usability_issues: Slight degradation of TAR and increase in FRR as the number of users in the system increases

key_contribution: Demonstrated that weight-balancing in XGBoost outperforms SMOTE and RUS for wearable biometric authentication
Validated scalability of the proposed system from 10 to 100 users
Identified pedometer features as highly significant for user identification
main_weakness: Data collected only in a controlled lab setting
Lack of active spoofing/presentation attack testing
insights.notes_for_review: Paper highlights that while accuracy remains high, F1-score and TAR decline as the population size grows, emphasizing the need for personalized models.
